text: This review focuses on the power of in situ UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectroscopy in tackling the complexity of catalytic systems. This review discusses various experimental methods which have been employed for the elucidation of catalytic reaction mechanisms and kinetic studies, and the structure characterization of enzymes, as well as heterogeneous and homogeneous catalysts in catalytic reactions such as photocatalysis. Experimental methods ranging from stopped-flow to laser-driven transient absorption as well as other methods involving perturbation have been employed for investigations over wide ranges of timescales applicable to almost all types of catalytic systems. In the case of photocatalysis, femto- and nanosecond-resolved experiments of the excited state species are often necessary for understanding the mechanisms and thus shorter timescale information are derived experimentally. The characterization of structure in catalysts is often also challenging as multi-spectroscopic approach are required. A combination of techniques such as magnetic circular dichroism and resonance Raman has enabled detailed characterization of metal centers in certain metalloenzymes, while characterization of the active sites of transition-metal-exchanged zeolites such as zeolite ZSM-5 required *in situ* MCD and Resonance Raman studies. Information such as the redox states of electrocatalysts, the oxygen vacancies on the surface of heterogeneous catalysts such as MOFs, sulfides, and perovskites and the band gaps of different nanomaterials especially those associated with the heterogeneous catalysts including those with electrocatalytic function is as well provided and highlighted as such in this review. Finally, in spite of the difficulties of broadband peaks associated with various species often attached to the surfaces of heterogeneous catalysts, which often leads to not tracking the occurrence of catalytic reaction on the surface, various strategies that have been employed in understanding various surface catalytic phenomena through various experiments involving *in situ* UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectroscopy have shown great promises of which needs further exploration in catalysts science.
